agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A feature request proposes adding a 'confirmed' status to the Quotes bounded context. The term 'confirmed' already has an established, enforced meaning in the Orders bounded context: paid and immutable. The new usage means 'verbally agreed, not yet paid' — the semantic opposite on the payment dimension.","bounded_context":"Quotes (proposed change) vs Orders (existing definition)","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Confirmed Order","definition":"EXISTING — An order that has been paid and is queued for fulfillment. It is immutable once confirmed."},{"term":"Confirmed Quote (proposed)","definition":"PROPOSED — A quote where the customer has verbally agreed to terms but payment has NOT yet occurred. Mutable status (can still fall through)."},{"term":"Confirmation","definition":"COLLISION — In Orders, confirmation = payment received + immutability. In the proposed Quote usage, confirmation = verbal agreement, no payment, no immutability. These are fundamentally different state transitions sharing the same word."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A confirmed order has been paid and is queued for fulfillment.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Payment receipt is a physical/financial fact. If an order is marked confirmed without payment, the system state is corrupt — fulfillment would proceed without revenue captured.","testable_assertion":"Every order in 'confirmed' status MUST have a corresponding successful payment record."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"A confirmed order is immutable.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Once paid and queued for fulfillment, changes would break downstream warehouse, shipping, and accounting processes. Immutability is a data consistency constraint.","testable_assertion":"No field on a confirmed order may be modified after it enters 'confirmed' status."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"A 'confirmed' quote (as proposed) represents verbal agreement without payment and is NOT immutable.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a sales workflow policy — a VP could change what 'verbal agreement' means or what actions are allowed on such quotes. It is a soft business policy, not a data integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"A quote in 'confirmed' status has no payment record and its terms can still be modified or the quote can be cancelled."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A developer queries all 'confirmed' entities across contexts and conflates paid orders with unpaid quotes.","expected_behavior":"Cross-context reporting must distinguish between confirmed orders (paid, immutable) and confirmed quotes (verbal agreement, mutable, unpaid). Using the same term makes this error inevitable."},{"scenario":"A customer support agent sees 'confirmed' on a quote and assumes payment has been collected, then tells the customer their order is being fulfilled.","expected_behavior":"The system must make it unambiguous that a confirmed quote is NOT paid. But sharing the term 'confirmed' actively works against this."},{"scenario":"A quote moves to 'confirmed', then an order is created from it and also moves to 'confirmed' — the audit trail shows two 'confirmed' transitions with completely different business meanings.","expected_behavior":"Audit and compliance systems must be able to distinguish these events. Shared terminology creates ambiguity in logs, reports, and dispute resolution."},{"scenario":"Integration partners or APIs consume a 'confirmed' webhook event and cannot determine if payment occurred.","expected_behavior":"Event consumers must not need to inspect the entity type to determine whether 'confirmed' means paid or unpaid. This violates the principle of least surprise."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Moderate risk. If the term collision leads any system or person to treat a verbal agreement as a paid commitment, fulfillment could begin without payment captured — direct revenue loss. Conversely, reporting that conflates the two statuses would misstate committed revenue vs pipeline.","operational":"High risk. Every team that touches both quotes and orders — sales, support, fulfillment, finance, engineering — must now hold two contradictory definitions of the same word in their heads. This is a guaranteed source of bugs, miscommunication, and process errors.","user":"Moderate risk. Sales agents may understand the distinction in their workflow, but customer support, finance, and API consumers will encounter confusion. The term 'confirmed' carries strong connotations of finality and payment in the order context.","risk":"High. This is a textbook ubiquitous language violation. Domain-Driven Design exists specifically to prevent this class of error. The immediate feature may work, but the term collision will compound over time as more code, reports, integrations, and team members interact with both contexts.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Sales Agents","impact":"Low — they understand a confirmed quote is verbal agreement in their workflow."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"High — they work across quotes AND orders; 'confirmed' meaning two different things will cause incorrect customer communication."},{"group":"Finance / Accounting","impact":"High — confirmed must mean paid in their world. A 'confirmed' quote appearing in reports risks misstatement of revenue or receivables."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"High — shared term across bounded contexts leaks ambiguity into code, events, APIs, and database queries."},{"group":"Integration Partners","impact":"Medium — API consumers receiving 'confirmed' events must now disambiguate by entity type, increasing coupling and error potential."}]},"confidence":{"level":35,"reasoning":"The spec contains a direct contradiction: 'confirmed' is defined as paid+immutable in Orders, and the feature request redefines it as unpaid+mutable in Quotes. This is not ambiguity — it is a semantic collision. Confidence is low because shipping this as-specified will encode a contradiction into the domain language that affects every stakeholder. The feature request's INTENT is clear and valid; the TERM chosen is wrong.","high_confidence_areas":["The business need is real — sales teams need a status for 'customer verbally agreed'","The existing definition of 'confirmed' in Orders is established and invariant","The two definitions are semantically incompatible on the dimensions of payment and mutability"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the organization has other contexts where 'confirmed' is already overloaded","What the correct alternative term should be — this requires sales team input"],"assumptions":["The existing 'confirmed order' definition is actively enforced in code and understood by all teams","Quotes and Orders are separate bounded contexts but share reporting, support, and integration surfaces"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The term 'confirmed' is being assigned two mutually exclusive definitions. In Orders: paid + immutable. In Quotes (proposed): unpaid + mutable. These cannot coexist under the same term without corrupting the ubiquitous language. The feature request's intent (tracking verbal agreement) is valid, but the chosen status name directly contradicts an established invariant definition.","affected_stakeholders":["Customer Support","Finance","Engineering","Integration Partners"],"options":["A: Use 'confirmed' for quotes as requested — accept the semantic collision and rely on context to disambiguate.","B: Rename the quote status to a distinct term that accurately reflects its meaning — e.g., 'accepted', 'verbally_agreed', 'customer_approved', or 'terms_accepted' — preserving 'confirmed' exclusively for its established meaning of paid+immutable.","C: Introduce a qualifier pattern — 'quote_confirmed' vs 'order_confirmed' — to namespace the term while retaining the word."],"recommendation":"Option B. Rename the quote status. The term 'accepted' or 'customer_approved' accurately describes verbal agreement without payment, avoids collision with the established invariant, and requires zero changes to existing order logic. This is the DDD-correct resolution: each bounded context should have unambiguous terms, and when contexts share a surface (reporting, support, APIs), terms must not collide. The cost of renaming now is trivial; the cost of the collision compounds permanently."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Ubiquitous language collision: 'confirmed' is redefined with contradictory semantics across bounded contexts that share operational surfaces.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 and BR-2 — the established definition of 'confirmed' implies payment and immutability. Reusing the term for an unpaid, mutable state undermines these invariants at the language level, even if the code keeps them technically separate."}]}
